name: Personal Finance / Applied Economics
nameZh: 个人理财与应用经济学
description: A practical course covering personal financial planning, investing, taxes, insurance, and the economic forces that shape everyday financial decisions.

modules.title: Financial Goal Setting & Mindset
modules.objectives: Define short-term, medium-term, and long-term financial goals Explain the difference between a scarcity mindset and an abundance mindset Apply SMART goal framework to a personal financial goal
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Personal Finance — browse the overview and goal-setting topics.
modules.videoNote: Introduction to Personal Finance and Financial Goal Setting.
modules.video2Note: SMART Goals explained with personal finance examples.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the introductory personal finance exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Write three SMART financial goals: one short-term (under 1 year), one medium-term (1–5 years), and one long-term (5+ years). Explain how each goal aligns with your values and how you will measure progress.

modules.title: Budgeting & Spending Plans
modules.objectives: Distinguish between needs and wants Create a monthly budget using the 50/30/20 rule Identify common budgeting pitfalls and strategies to avoid them
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Budgeting and Financial Planning section.
modules.videoNote: How to make a budget and stick to it — step-by-step guide.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the budgeting practice exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Create a sample monthly budget for a young adult earning $2,500/month after taxes. Categorize all expenses, apply the 50/30/20 rule, and identify two areas where spending could be reduced.

modules.title: Banking, Savings & Emergency Funds
modules.objectives: Compare checking and savings accounts Explain how compound interest grows savings over time Calculate an appropriate emergency fund target
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Saving Money and Banking topics.
modules.videoNote: Banking basics: checking, savings, and how to choose the right account.
modules.video2Note: The power of compound interest and building an emergency fund.
modules.practiceNote: Work through the savings and compound interest practice problems.
modules.assignment: Using the compound interest formula A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), calculate the value of saving $200/month at 4% annual interest (compounded monthly) for 5, 10, and 20 years. Reflect on what your calculations reveal about the value of starting early.

modules.title: Credit, Debt & Credit Scores
modules.objectives: Explain how credit scores are calculated and what factors affect them Compare types of consumer debt (credit cards, auto loans, student loans) Describe strategies for managing and eliminating debt
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Loans and Debt section.
modules.videoNote: Understanding your credit score: what it is and how to improve it.
modules.video2Note: Credit card debt: how interest works and how to pay it off faster.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the loans and debt exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Analyze a scenario where a person has $5,000 in credit card debt at 22% APR. Calculate the monthly interest charge and develop a debt payoff plan using the avalanche method. Then compare it with the snowball method and explain which you would choose and why.

modules.title: Introduction to Investing
modules.objectives: Explain the risk-return tradeoff Identify major asset classes: stocks, bonds, real estate, and cash equivalents Describe the principle of diversification
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Investment Vehicles, Insurance, and Retirement section.
modules.videoNote: Investing 101: stocks, bonds, and the basics of growing wealth.
modules.video2Note: Risk vs. return: how to think about investment risk as a beginner.
modules.practiceNote: Work through the investment basics exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Build a hypothetical beginner investment portfolio with $10,000. Allocate the money across at least three different asset classes. Explain each allocation choice using the concepts of risk tolerance, time horizon, and diversification.

modules.title: Stock Market Basics
modules.objectives: Explain how stock markets function and how stocks are priced Distinguish between market orders, limit orders, and other order types Interpret basic stock information: P/E ratio, dividend yield, market capitalization
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Stocks and Bonds core finance section.
modules.videoNote: How does the stock market work? A beginner's guide.
modules.video2Note: How to read a stock quote and understand key metrics.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the stocks and bonds practice exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Research two publicly traded companies of your choice. Compare their P/E ratios, dividend yields, and 52-week price range. Write a brief analysis of which company you would invest in and justify your reasoning using at least three financial metrics.

modules.title: Bonds, Mutual Funds & ETFs
modules.objectives: Explain how bonds work and the relationship between bond prices and interest rates Compare mutual funds and ETFs as investment vehicles Calculate a simple bond yield
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Bonds section within Stocks and Bonds.
modules.videoNote: Bonds explained: how they work and when to invest in them.
modules.video2Note: ETFs vs. mutual funds: what's the difference and which is better for beginners?
modules.practiceNote: Complete the bonds and investment vehicles exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: A bond has a face value of $1,000, a coupon rate of 5%, and matures in 10 years. The current market interest rate rises to 7%. Explain qualitatively and quantitatively (estimate) how the bond's market price is affected. Then compare investing $5,000 in this bond versus a low-cost S&P 500 ETF over 10 years.

modules.title: Retirement Planning (401k, IRA)
modules.objectives: Explain the difference between traditional and Roth retirement accounts Describe the tax advantages of 401k and IRA accounts Calculate the long-term value of consistent retirement contributions
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Retirement section within Investment Vehicles.
modules.videoNote: 401k vs. IRA: which is better for you?
modules.video2Note: Traditional vs. Roth IRA: tax strategies for retirement.
modules.practiceNote: Work through the retirement planning exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Compare two retirement scenarios: Person A contributes $300/month to a Roth IRA starting at age 22; Person B contributes $500/month starting at age 35. Assume 7% average annual return. Calculate both balances at age 65 and write a reflection on the importance of starting early.

modules.title: Taxes & Filing Basics
modules.objectives: Explain the US progressive income tax system Distinguish between gross income, adjusted gross income, and taxable income Describe the purpose of W-2, 1040, and common tax deductions/credits
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Taxes section of Personal Finance.
modules.videoNote: How the US tax system works: a complete beginner's guide.
modules.video2Note: How to read your W-2 and file your first tax return.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the tax calculation exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Given a taxable income of $48,000 and the 2024 US federal tax brackets, calculate the total federal income tax owed using the marginal tax rate system. Then identify two tax credits or deductions a young adult might qualify for and explain how they reduce the tax burden.

modules.title: Insurance (Health, Auto, Life)
modules.objectives: Explain the purpose of insurance as a risk management tool Compare health, auto, and life insurance types and key terms (premium, deductible, copay) Evaluate when and how much insurance coverage is appropriate
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Insurance section within Investment Vehicles and Insurance.
modules.videoNote: Insurance basics: how insurance works and why you need it.
modules.video2Note: Health insurance explained: premiums, deductibles, copays, and out-of-pocket maximums.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the insurance exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Compare two health insurance plans: Plan A has a $200/month premium and $1,000 deductible; Plan B has a $100/month premium and $3,500 deductible. In what scenarios is each plan more cost-effective? Calculate the break-even point and recommend a plan for a healthy 22-year-old with no chronic conditions.

modules.title: Housing & Real Estate
modules.objectives: Compare renting versus buying a home using financial criteria Explain the key costs of homeownership: mortgage, property tax, insurance, maintenance Calculate a simple mortgage payment and total interest paid
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Housing section of Personal Finance.
modules.videoNote: Rent vs. buy: how to make the right decision for your situation.
modules.video2Note: How mortgages work: fixed vs. adjustable rate, amortization explained.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the housing and mortgage calculation exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Compare renting an apartment for $1,200/month versus buying a $250,000 home with a 10% down payment at 6.5% interest over 30 years. Use a mortgage calculator to find the monthly payment. Factor in property taxes ($3,000/year), insurance ($1,200/year), and maintenance (1% of home value/year). Which option is more cost-effective over 10 years?

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of a long-term financial goal?
options: Saving $200 for a new phone next month
Paying off a credit card balance in 3 months
Saving for a down payment on a house in 7 years
Buying groceries for the week
quizQuestions.answer: Saving for a down payment on a house in 7 years
quizQuestions.explanation: Long-term financial goals typically span more than 5 years. Saving for a house down payment in 7 years fits this definition.

quizQuestions.question: Which characteristic makes a financial goal 'SMART'?
options: It must involve investing in stocks
It must be specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound
It must be set by a financial advisor
It must always focus on eliminating debt
quizQuestions.answer: It must be specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound
quizQuestions.explanation: SMART goals are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound — a framework that improves goal clarity and success rates.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is an example of a long-term financial goal" is: Saving for a down payment on a house in 7 years.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Saving for a down payment on a house in 7 years is correct.

quizQuestions.question: According to the 50/30/20 budgeting rule, what percentage of after-tax income should go toward wants?
quizQuestions.explanation: The 50/30/20 rule allocates 50% to needs, 30% to wants, and 20% to savings and debt repayment.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is considered a 'need' in a budget?
options: Streaming services subscription
Restaurant dining
Rent or mortgage payment
New video game
quizQuestions.answer: Rent or mortgage payment
quizQuestions.explanation: Needs are essential expenses required for basic living, such as housing, food, utilities, and transportation. Rent is a fundamental need.

quizQuestions.question: What is zero-based budgeting?
options: Spending nothing during the month
Allocating every dollar of income to specific categories so income minus expenses equals zero
Saving 100% of your income
A budgeting method that requires no tracking
quizQuestions.answer: Allocating every dollar of income to specific categories so income minus expenses equals zero
quizQuestions.explanation: Zero-based budgeting assigns every dollar of income a specific purpose, ensuring that income minus all allocations (including savings) equals zero.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "According to the 50/30/20 budgeting rule, what percentage of after-tax income should go toward wants" is: 30%.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. 30% is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary advantage of a high-yield savings account over a standard savings account?
options: It offers guaranteed stock market returns
It charges no monthly fees
It offers a significantly higher annual percentage yield (APY)
It has no withdrawal limits
quizQuestions.answer: It offers a significantly higher annual percentage yield (APY)
quizQuestions.explanation: High-yield savings accounts typically offer APYs that are many times higher than standard savings accounts, allowing your money to grow faster.

quizQuestions.question: How many months of living expenses is commonly recommended for an emergency fund?
options: 1 month
3–6 months
10–12 months
24 months
quizQuestions.answer: 3–6 months
quizQuestions.explanation: Financial experts generally recommend maintaining 3–6 months of essential living expenses in an easily accessible emergency fund.

quizQuestions.question: With compound interest, interest is calculated on:
options: Only the original principal
Only new deposits made each month
The original principal plus all previously earned interest
The account balance minus fees
quizQuestions.answer: The original principal plus all previously earned interest
quizQuestions.explanation: Compound interest is interest on interest. Each period, interest is earned not just on the principal but also on all interest accumulated so far.

quizQuestions.question: Which factor has the greatest impact on a credit score?
options: Length of credit history
Types of credit used
Payment history
New credit inquiries
quizQuestions.answer: Payment history
quizQuestions.explanation: Payment history is the most significant factor in credit scoring, accounting for approximately 35% of a FICO score.

quizQuestions.question: What is the annual percentage rate (APR) on a credit card?
options: The monthly fee charged for having the card
The annualized interest rate charged on outstanding balances
The maximum credit limit on the card
The total amount you can borrow per year
quizQuestions.answer: The annualized interest rate charged on outstanding balances
quizQuestions.explanation: APR represents the yearly cost of borrowing on a credit card, expressed as a percentage of the outstanding balance.

quizQuestions.question: The debt avalanche method prioritizes paying off:
options: The smallest debt balance first to build momentum
The debt with the lowest interest rate first
The debt with the highest interest rate first
All debts equally at the same time
quizQuestions.answer: The debt with the highest interest rate first
quizQuestions.explanation: The avalanche method targets the highest-interest debt first, minimizing total interest paid over time — the most mathematically efficient approach.

quizQuestions.question: A credit utilization ratio of 80% would likely:
options: Significantly improve your credit score
Have no effect on your credit score
Significantly lower your credit score
Qualify you for a lower interest rate
quizQuestions.answer: Significantly lower your credit score
quizQuestions.explanation: Credit utilization above 30% negatively affects credit scores. An 80% utilization ratio signals high dependence on credit and lowers scores significantly.

quizQuestions.question: The risk-return tradeoff in investing means:
options: Higher risk always guarantees higher returns
Investments with higher potential returns generally carry higher risk
Lower-risk investments always outperform higher-risk ones
There is no relationship between risk and potential return
quizQuestions.answer: Investments with higher potential returns generally carry higher risk
quizQuestions.explanation: The fundamental principle of the risk-return tradeoff is that to earn higher potential returns, investors must accept higher levels of risk.

quizQuestions.question: Diversification in an investment portfolio means:
options: Putting all your money in one high-performing stock
Spreading investments across different asset classes and sectors to reduce risk
Only investing in government bonds
Investing the same dollar amount every month
quizQuestions.answer: Spreading investments across different asset classes and sectors to reduce risk
quizQuestions.explanation: Diversification reduces the impact of any single investment's poor performance on the overall portfolio by spreading risk across many assets.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The risk-return tradeoff in investing means:" is: Investments with higher potential returns generally carry higher risk.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Investments with higher potential returns generally carry higher risk is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Diversification in an investment portfolio means:" is: Putting all your money in one high-performing stock.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Spreading investments across different asset classes and sectors to reduce risk.

quizQuestions.question: A company's market capitalization is calculated as:
options: Annual revenue minus annual expenses
Share price multiplied by total shares outstanding
Total assets minus total liabilities
Net income divided by shares outstanding
quizQuestions.answer: Share price multiplied by total shares outstanding
quizQuestions.explanation: Market capitalization = Share Price × Total Shares Outstanding. It represents the total market value of a company's equity.

quizQuestions.question: A stock with a P/E ratio of 5 compared to the industry average of 20 could indicate:
options: The stock is overvalued relative to its peers
The stock is undervalued or the company faces earnings concerns
The company has no debt
The stock will definitely increase in price
quizQuestions.answer: The stock is undervalued or the company faces earnings concerns
quizQuestions.explanation: A low P/E ratio relative to peers can indicate undervaluation (a potential buying opportunity) or it may reflect investor concerns about future earnings.

quizQuestions.question: A limit order to buy a stock at $50 means:
options: You will buy the stock immediately at whatever price it is trading at
You will buy the stock only if the price falls to $50 or below
You will buy the stock only if the price rises above $50
You will buy exactly 50 shares of the stock
quizQuestions.answer: You will buy the stock only if the price falls to $50 or below
quizQuestions.explanation: A buy limit order executes only at the specified price or lower, giving investors price control but no guarantee of execution.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A company's market capitalization is calculated as:" is: Share price multiplied by total shares outstanding.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Share price multiplied by total shares outstanding is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "which index is most commonly used to measure the overall performance of the us stock market."
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: S&P 500.

quizQuestions.question: When interest rates in the economy rise, existing bond prices generally:
options: Rise proportionally
Fall
Stay the same
Double
quizQuestions.answer: Fall
quizQuestions.explanation: Bond prices and interest rates have an inverse relationship. When rates rise, existing bonds with lower coupon rates become less attractive, so their prices fall.

quizQuestions.question: What is an ETF (Exchange-Traded Fund)?
options: A type of savings account that trades on exchanges
A fund that tracks an index or basket of assets and trades on stock exchanges like a stock
A government-issued bond sold through exchanges
A type of money market account
quizQuestions.answer: A fund that tracks an index or basket of assets and trades on stock exchanges like a stock
quizQuestions.explanation: ETFs combine the diversification of a mutual fund with the trading flexibility of a stock, typically at low costs.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "When interest rates in the economy rise, existing bond prices generally:" is: Fall.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Fall is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is the main tax advantage of a traditional 401(k) plan?
options: Contributions are made with after-tax dollars; withdrawals are tax-free
Contributions reduce your taxable income in the year they are made
There are no limits on annual contributions
The employer must match 100% of your contributions
quizQuestions.answer: Contributions reduce your taxable income in the year they are made
quizQuestions.explanation: Traditional 401(k) contributions are made pre-tax, reducing current taxable income. Taxes are paid upon withdrawal in retirement.

quizQuestions.question: A Roth IRA differs from a traditional IRA primarily because:
options: Roth contributions are tax-deductible; traditional are not
Roth withdrawals in retirement are tax-free; traditional withdrawals are taxed
Roth IRAs have higher contribution limits
Roth IRAs are only available through employers
quizQuestions.answer: Roth withdrawals in retirement are tax-free; traditional withdrawals are taxed
quizQuestions.explanation: Roth IRA contributions are made with after-tax dollars, so qualified withdrawals in retirement (including gains) are completely tax-free.

quizQuestions.question: What is an employer 401(k) match and why is it valuable?
options: The employer charges a fee equal to your contribution
The employer contributes an amount to your 401(k) based on your own contributions — essentially free money
The employer invests your contributions in stocks without your input
The employer takes over managing your retirement account
quizQuestions.answer: The employer contributes an amount to your 401(k) based on your own contributions — essentially free money
quizQuestions.explanation: An employer match is free compensation that immediately boosts your retirement savings. Not contributing enough to get the full match means leaving money on the table.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The main tax advantage of a traditional 401(k) plan is "Contributions reduce your taxable income in the year they are made".
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Contributions reduce your taxable income in the year they are made is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The US federal income tax system is progressive, which means:
options: Everyone pays the same tax rate regardless of income
Higher income is taxed at higher marginal rates
Tax rates decrease as income increases
Taxes are only paid by corporations
quizQuestions.answer: Higher income is taxed at higher marginal rates
quizQuestions.explanation: In a progressive tax system, income is taxed at higher rates as it moves into higher tax brackets — but only the income within each bracket is taxed at that rate.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of a W-2 form?
options: To report freelance income
To report wages earned and taxes withheld by an employer
To claim tax deductions for self-employment
To report investment income
quizQuestions.answer: To report wages earned and taxes withheld by an employer
quizQuestions.explanation: A W-2 is issued by employers and shows total wages paid and taxes withheld, used by employees to file their annual federal and state tax returns.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between a tax deduction and a tax credit?
options: A deduction reduces taxes owed dollar-for-dollar; a credit reduces taxable income
A credit reduces taxes owed dollar-for-dollar; a deduction reduces taxable income
They are the same thing
Deductions are only for businesses; credits are only for individuals
quizQuestions.answer: A credit reduces taxes owed dollar-for-dollar; a deduction reduces taxable income
quizQuestions.explanation: Tax deductions lower your taxable income (saving you taxes at your marginal rate); tax credits reduce the actual taxes you owe dollar-for-dollar — making credits generally more valuable.

quizQuestions.question: What is an insurance premium?
options: The amount you pay out-of-pocket when you file a claim
The regular payment (monthly or annually) you make to keep an insurance policy active
The maximum amount an insurance company will pay
The penalty for canceling a policy early
quizQuestions.answer: The regular payment (monthly or annually) you make to keep an insurance policy active
quizQuestions.explanation: A premium is the regular payment required to maintain insurance coverage, regardless of whether you file any claims.

quizQuestions.question: A health insurance deductible is:
options: The total amount the insurance company will pay for medical bills
The amount you must pay out-of-pocket before insurance begins covering costs
The monthly premium for your health plan
The percentage of costs you share with the insurance company after the deductible
quizQuestions.answer: The amount you must pay out-of-pocket before insurance begins covering costs
quizQuestions.explanation: The deductible is the amount you pay before your insurance kicks in. Plans with higher deductibles typically have lower premiums.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of life insurance provides coverage for a specific period of time and is generally the most affordable?
options: Whole life insurance
Universal life insurance
Variable life insurance
Term life insurance
quizQuestions.answer: Term life insurance
quizQuestions.explanation: Term life insurance covers a specific period (e.g., 20 years) and pays a death benefit only if the insured dies during that term. It is typically the lowest-cost form of life insurance.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of moral hazard in insurance refers to:
options: Dishonest claims made by policyholders
The tendency to take more risks because you are insured
Insurance companies refusing to pay legitimate claims
The process of assessing risk before issuing a policy
quizQuestions.answer: The tendency to take more risks because you are insured
quizQuestions.explanation: Moral hazard occurs when having insurance reduces the incentive to avoid risks — e.g., driving less carefully because you have auto insurance.

quizQuestions.question: When comparing renting versus buying a home, which factor favors renting?
options: Building equity over time
Stability and permanence
Lower upfront costs and flexibility to relocate
Tax deductions for mortgage interest
quizQuestions.answer: Lower upfront costs and flexibility to relocate
quizQuestions.explanation: Renting requires much less upfront capital and allows greater mobility — advantages especially relevant for young professionals or those with uncertain location plans.

quizQuestions.question: What is amortization in the context of a mortgage?
options: The process of paying down a mortgage through regular payments that cover both principal and interest
The penalty charged for paying off a mortgage early
The increase in home value over time
The property tax calculated each year
quizQuestions.answer: The process of paying down a mortgage through regular payments that cover both principal and interest
quizQuestions.explanation: Amortization is the gradual reduction of a mortgage loan balance through scheduled payments, with early payments weighted more toward interest and later payments more toward principal.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "When comparing renting versus buying a home, which factor favors renting" is: Lower upfront costs and flexibility to relocate.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Lower upfront costs and flexibility to relocate is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: Amortization in the context of a mortgage is "The penalty charged for paying off a mortgage early".
options: True
False
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The process of paying down a mortgage through regular payments that cover both principal and interest.

questions.question: A SMART financial goal requires that the goal be all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Specific and clearly defined
Achievable given current resources
Approved by a financial advisor
Time-bound with a clear deadline
questions.explanation: SMART stands for Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. Advisor approval is not one of the SMART criteria.

questions.question: Using the 50/30/20 budgeting rule on a $3,000 monthly take-home income, how much should be allocated to savings and debt repayment?
questions.explanation: The 20% savings/debt repayment allocation: 20% × $3,000 = $600.

questions.question: You deposit $5,000 in an account earning 6% annual interest compounded annually. Approximately how much will it be worth after 2 years?
True or False: The answer to "Which FICO credit score range is generally considered 'good' by most lenders" is: 670–739.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: True. 670–739 is correct.

questions.question: What type of risk CANNOT be eliminated through diversification?
options: Company-specific risk
Industry risk
Systematic (market) risk
Management risk
questions.explanation: Systematic risk affects the entire market and cannot be diversified away. Unsystematic (company or industry specific) risk can be reduced through diversification.

questions.question: A company reports earnings per share (EPS) of $4. Its stock trades at $60. What is the price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio?
questions.explanation: P/E Ratio = Stock Price / EPS = $60 / $4 = 15.

questions.question: A bond with a face value of $1,000 and a coupon rate of 4% pays how much in annual interest?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: $40.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The debt snowball method differs from the debt avalanche method in that snowball prioritizes:" is: Debts with the highest interest rate first.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Debts with the smallest balance first.

questions.question: If a savings account pays 3% APY and inflation is 5%, the real return on the savings is:
questions.explanation: Real return ≈ Nominal Rate − Inflation Rate = 3% − 5% = −2%. The account loses purchasing power in real terms.

questions.question: Which retirement account type allows tax-free growth AND tax-free withdrawals in retirement?
options: Traditional 401(k)
Traditional IRA
Roth IRA
Pension plan
questions.explanation: Roth IRA contributions are made with after-tax dollars. Qualified withdrawals in retirement — including all investment gains — are completely tax-free.

questions.question: The standard deduction for a single filer reduces taxable income by a fixed amount. If the standard deduction is $14,600 and gross income is $55,000, taxable income equals:
questions.explanation: Taxable income = Gross Income − Standard Deduction = $55,000 − $14,600 = $40,400.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "An emergency fund should primarily be kept in which type of account" is: High-yield savings account or money market account.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: True. High-yield savings account or money market account is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a certificate of deposit (CD)?
options: A type of stock that pays dividends
A savings instrument that pays a fixed interest rate for a specified term, with penalties for early withdrawal
A government bond with a 30-year maturity
A mutual fund that invests in certificates
questions.answer: A savings instrument that pays a fixed interest rate for a specified term, with penalties for early withdrawal
questions.explanation: CDs offer higher interest rates than regular savings accounts in exchange for locking up funds for a specific term. Early withdrawal typically incurs a penalty.

questions.question: A bank charges 24% APR on a credit card balance. If you carry a $1,000 balance for one month, approximately how much interest do you owe?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: $20.

questions.question: Which factor is NOT typically part of a FICO credit score calculation?
options: Payment history
Credit utilization ratio
Annual income
Length of credit history
questions.explanation: FICO scores are based on payment history, amounts owed, length of credit history, new credit, and credit mix. Income is not a factor in the FICO score calculation.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following demonstrates an abundance mindset in personal finance" is: Believing you will never have enough money to invest.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Viewing money as a tool for creating opportunities and giving generously.

questions.question: You earn $4,000/month after taxes. Following the 50/30/20 rule, what is your monthly wants budget?
You invest $2,000 at 8% compounded annually. Approximately how long will it take to double?
options: 6 years
9 years
12 years
16 years
questions.explanation: Using the Rule of 72: 72 / 8 = 9 years.

questions.question: A person pays $1,800/year in auto insurance premiums and has a $500 deductible. They file a claim for $2,500 in damage. How much does insurance pay?
questions.explanation: The insured pays the $500 deductible first; insurance covers the remainder: $2,500 − $500 = $2,000.

questions.question: True or False: The expense ratio of an ETF is "The annual fee charged by the fund as a percentage of assets under management".
options: True
False
questions.explanation: True. The annual fee charged by the fund as a percentage of assets under management is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the Federal Reserve's primary tool for controlling inflation?
options: Printing more money
Adjusting the federal funds interest rate
Raising income taxes
Regulating stock market trading
questions.answer: Adjusting the federal funds interest rate
questions.explanation: The Federal Reserve primarily uses the federal funds rate to influence economic activity. Raising rates makes borrowing more expensive, reducing spending and inflation.

questions.question: A homeowner with a $300,000 home and a $220,000 remaining mortgage has home equity of:
questions.explanation: Home equity = Market Value − Outstanding Mortgage = $300,000 − $220,000 = $80,000.

questions.question: Which statement about Social Security and self-employment tax is correct?
options: Self-employed individuals pay no Social Security taxes
Self-employed individuals pay only the employee's share (7.65%)
Self-employed individuals pay the full 15.3% (both employer and employee shares)
Social Security taxes only apply to incomes over $200,000
questions.answer: Self-employed individuals pay the full 15.3% (both employer and employee shares)
questions.explanation: Self-employed individuals bear both the employer (7.65%) and employee (7.65%) portions of payroll taxes, totaling 15.3%, though half is deductible.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Stagflation is defined as a situation in which:" is: The economy experiences rapid GDP growth with zero inflation.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: High inflation occurs simultaneously with slow economic growth and high unemployment.

questions.question: What is the maximum annual contribution limit to a Roth IRA for individuals under 50 in 2024?
questions.explanation: In 2024, the IRS allows individuals under 50 to contribute up to $7,000 per year to IRAs (traditional or Roth combined).

questions.question: An investor practices dollar-cost averaging by investing $400/month in an index fund. When prices are low, this strategy results in:
options: Investing less money that month
Buying fewer shares that month
Buying more shares that month
Skipping the investment that month
questions.explanation: With DCA, the fixed dollar amount buys more shares when prices are low and fewer shares when prices are high, automatically achieving a lower average cost per share.

questions.question: Term life insurance is best suited for someone who:
options: Wants permanent coverage and a cash value component
Needs affordable coverage to protect dependents during peak earning and obligation years
Wants to use life insurance primarily as an investment vehicle
Has no dependents and no debts
questions.answer: Needs affordable coverage to protect dependents during peak earning and obligation years
questions.explanation: Term life insurance provides affordable death benefit protection for a specific period, ideal for families with mortgages, young children, or income replacement needs.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "What does the acronym APY stand for and how does it differ from APR" is: Annual Percentage Yield — it includes the effect of compounding; APR does not.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: True. Annual Percentage Yield — it includes the effect of compounding; APR does not is correct.

questions.question: Which personal finance action has the greatest positive long-term impact for a 22-year-old?
options: Waiting until age 40 to start investing to accumulate a larger lump sum
Starting to invest a modest amount consistently as early as possible
Focusing entirely on paying off all debt before saving anything
Keeping all savings in a checking account for easy access
questions.answer: Starting to invest a modest amount consistently as early as possible
questions.explanation: Compound growth is most powerful over long time periods. Starting early — even with small amounts — dramatically outperforms larger contributions started later.

questions.question: If real GDP per capita is growing while inflation is low and unemployment is falling, the economy is most likely in a:
options: Recession
Period of economic expansion or boom
Stagflation environment
Deflationary spiral
questions.answer: Period of economic expansion or boom
questions.explanation: Rising real GDP per capita, falling unemployment, and low inflation are classic indicators of economic expansion — a healthy, growing economy.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of private mortgage insurance (PMI)?
options: To insure the homeowner's belongings inside the house
To protect the lender if the borrower defaults when the down payment is less than 20%
To guarantee the home's value will not decrease
To cover property taxes if the homeowner cannot pay
questions.answer: To protect the lender if the borrower defaults when the down payment is less than 20%
questions.explanation: PMI protects the lender (not the borrower) against losses if the borrower defaults. It is required when loan-to-value exceeds 80% (down payment less than 20%).

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A business earns $80,000 in revenue and has total costs (fixed + variable) of $65,000. Its profit margin is approximately:" is: 81.25%.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: 18.75%.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why a Roth IRA may be preferable for a 22-year-old with a currently low income?
options: It offers immediate tax deductions on contributions
Paying taxes now at a lower rate and withdrawing tax-free in retirement (when income may be higher) maximizes long-term benefit
It has no contribution limits unlike traditional IRAs
Roth IRA contributions can be deducted from state taxes
questions.answer: Paying taxes now at a lower rate and withdrawing tax-free in retirement (when income may be higher) maximizes long-term benefit
questions.explanation: Young, lower-income earners are in lower tax brackets. Paying tax now (Roth) and avoiding taxes later (when bracket may be higher) is often the optimal strategy.

questions.question: What is the difference between systematic and unsystematic investment risk?
options: Systematic risk applies only to bonds; unsystematic risk applies only to stocks
Systematic risk affects individual companies; unsystematic risk affects the whole market
Systematic risk affects the entire market and cannot be diversified away; unsystematic risk is specific to a company or industry and can be reduced through diversification
They are the same concept described differently
questions.answer: Systematic risk affects the entire market and cannot be diversified away; unsystematic risk is specific to a company or industry and can be reduced through diversification
questions.explanation: Systematic risk (market risk) includes recessions and interest rate changes — unavoidable. Unsystematic (specific) risk can be reduced by holding a diversified portfolio.

questions.question: A comprehensive personal financial plan should be reviewed and updated:
options: Once, when it is first created
Only during a financial crisis
Regularly (annually or when major life changes occur) to reflect changing goals, income, and circumstances
Only when a financial advisor recommends it
questions.answer: Regularly (annually or when major life changes occur) to reflect changing goals, income, and circumstances
questions.explanation: Financial plans should be living documents reviewed at least annually and updated whenever major life events occur (job change, marriage, children, home purchase) to remain relevant.
